analogy: Think of "Alternating Current" through the chapter's own opening images: 7.1 INTRODUCTION We have so far considered direct current (dc) sources and circuits with dc sources. These currents do not change direction with time. But voltages and currents that vary with time are very common. The electric mains supply in our homes and offices is... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 7.1 INTRODUCTION, ALTERNATING, CURRENT, NICOLA TESLA (1856 – 1943). Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Alternating Current is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 7.1 INTRODUCTION We have so far considered direct current (dc) sources and circuits with dc sources.

The text explains that these currents do not change direction with time.

The text explains that but voltages and currents that vary with time are very common.

The text explains that the electric mains supply in our homes and offices is a voltage that varies like a sine function with time.

The text explains that such a voltage is called alternating voltage (ac voltage) and the current driven by it in a circuit is called the alternating current (ac current)*.

The text explains that today, most of the electrical devices we use require ac voltage.

The text explains that this is mainly because most of the electrical energy sold by power companies is transmitted and distributed as alternating current.

The text explains that the main reason for preferring use of ac voltage over dc voltage is that ac voltages can be easily and efficiently converted from one voltage to the other by means of transformers.

The text explains that further, electrical energy can also be transmitted economically over long distances.

The text explains that aC circuits exhibit characteristics which are exploited in many devices of daily use.

The text explains that for example, whenever we tune our radio to a favourite station, we are taking advantage of a special property of ac circuits – one of many that you will study in this chapter.

The text explains that chapter Seven ALTERNATING CURRENT * The phrases ac voltage and ac current are contradictory and redundant, respectively, since they mean, literally, alternating current voltage and alternating current current.

The text explains that still, the abbreviation ac to designate an electrical quantity displaying simple harmonic time dependance has become so universally accepted that we follow others in its use.

The text explains that further, voltage – another phrase commonly used means potential difference between two points.

The text explains that physics 178 NICOLA TESLA (1856 – 1943) Nicola Tesla (1856 – 1943) Serbian-American scientist, inventor and genius.

The text explains that he conceived the idea of the rotating magnetic field, which is the basis of practically all alternating current machinery, and which helped usher in the age of electric power.

The text explains that he also invented among other things the induction motor, the polyphase system of ac power, and the high frequency induction coil (the Tesla coil) used in radio and television sets and other electronic equipment.

The text explains that 7.2 AC VOLTAGE APPLIED TO A RESISTOR Figure 7.1 shows a resistor connected to a source ε of ac voltage.

The text explains that the symbol for an ac source in a circuit diagram is .

The text explains that we consider a source which produces sinusoidally varying potential difference across its terminals.

The text explains that 179 Alternating Current GEORGE WESTINGHOUSE (1846 – 1914) George Westinghouse (1846 – 1914) A leading proponent of the use of alternating current over direct current.

The text explains that thus, he came into conflict with Thomas Alva Edison, an advocate of direct current.

The text explains that find (a) the resistance of the bulb; (b) the peak voltage of the source; and (c) the rms current through the bulb.

The text explains that the resistance of the bulb is ( )2 2 220 V 484 100 W V R P = = = Ω (b) The peak voltage of the source is V 2 311 m v V = = (c) Since, P = I V 100 W 0.454A 220 V    P I V EXAMPLE 7.1

The text explains that figure 7.4(a) shows the voltage and current phasors and their relationship at time t1 for the case of an ac source connected to a resistor i.e., corresponding to the circuit shown in Fig.

The text explains that 7.4(a) we see that phasors V and I for the case of a resistor are in the same direction.

The text explains that 7.4 AC VOLTAGE APPLIED TO AN INDUCTOR Figure 7.5 shows an ac source connected to an inductor.

The text explains that let the voltage across the source be v = vm sin w t.

The text explains that fIGURE 7.5 An ac source connected to an inductor. * Though voltage and current in ac circuit are represented by phasors – rotating vectors, they are not vectors themselves.

The text explains that it so happens that the amplitudes and phases of harmonically varying scalars combine mathematically in the same way as do the projections of rotating vectors of corresponding magnitudes and directions.

The text explains that the negative sign follows from Lenz’s law (Chapter 6).

The text explains that (7.1) and (7.10), we have d sin d m v i v t t L L ω = = (7.11) Equation (7.11) implies that the equation for i(t), the current as a function of time, must be such that its slope di/dt is a sinusoidally varying quantity, with the same phase as the source voltage and an amplitude given by vm/L.

The text explains that since the source has an emf which oscillates symmetrically about zero, the current it sustains also oscillates symmetrically about zero, so that no constant or time-independent component of the current exists.

The text explains that the inductive reactance is directly proportional to the inductance and to the frequency of the current.

The text explains that (7.1) and (7.12) for the source voltage and the current in an inductor shows that the current lags the voltage by p/2 or one-quarter (1/4) cycle.

The text explains that example 7.2 A pure inductor of 25.0 mH is connected to a source of 220 V.

The text explains that find the inductive reactance and rms current in the circuit if the frequency of the source is 50 Hz.

The text explains that physics 184 7.5 AC VOLTAGE APPLIED TO A CAPACITOR Figure 7.7 shows an ac source e generating ac voltage v = vm sin wt connected to a capacitor only, a purely capacitive ac circuit.

The text explains that when a capacitor is connected to a voltage source in a dc circuit, current will flow for the short time required to charge the capacitor.

The text explains that when the capacitor is connected to an ac source, as in Fig.

The text explains that the instantaneous voltage v across the capacitor is q v C = (7.15) From the Kirchhoff’s loop rule, the voltage across the source and the capacitor are equal, sin m q v t C ω = To find the current, we use the relation d d q i t = ( ) d d sin cos( ) m m i v C t C v t t ω ω ω = = Using the relation, cos( ) sin ω ω t t = +     π 2 , we have i i t m = +     sin ω π 2 (7.16) where the amplitude of the oscillating current is im = wCvm.

The text explains that it is called capacitive reactance and is denoted by Xc, Xc= 1/ wC (7.17) so that the amplitude of the current is m m C v i X = (7.18) FIGURE 7.7 An ac source connected to a capacitor.

The text explains that (7.16) with the equation of source voltage, Eq.

The text explains that solution When a dc source is connected to a capacitor, the capacitor gets charged and after charging no current flows in the circuit and the lamp will not glow.

The text explains that with ac source, the capacitor offers capacitative reactance (1/ ωC ) and the current flows in the circuit.

The text explains that reducing C will increase reactance and the lamp will shine less brightly than before.

The text explains that example 7.4 A 15.0 μF capacitor is connected to a 220 V, 50 Hz source.

The text explains that 7.5 In Exercises 7.3 and 7.4, what is the net power absorbed by each circuit over a complete cycle.

The text explains that 7.6 A charged 30 mF capacitor is connected to a 27 mH inductor.

The text explains that what is the angular frequency of free oscillations of the circuit?

The text explains that 7.7 A series LCR circuit with R = 20 W, L = 1.5 H and C = 35 mF is connected to a variable-frequency 200 V ac supply.

The text explains that when the frequency of the supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, what is the average power transferred to the circuit in one complete cycle?

revision_bullets: 7.1 INTRODUCTION We have so far considered direct current (dc) sources and circuits with dc sources. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
But voltages and currents that vary with time are very common. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
The electric mains supply in our homes and offices is a voltage that varies like a sine function with time. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
Such a voltage is called alternating voltage (ac voltage) and the current driven by it in a circuit is called the alternating current (ac current)*. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
Today, most of the electrical devices we use require ac voltage. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
This is mainly because most of the electrical energy sold by power companies is transmitted and distributed as alternating current. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
The main reason for preferring use of ac voltage over dc voltage is that ac voltages can be easily and efficiently converted from one voltage to... (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
Further, electrical energy can also be transmitted economically over long distances. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
AC circuits exhibit characteristics which are exploited in many devices of daily use. (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
For example, whenever we tune our radio to a favourite station, we are taking advantage of a special property of ac circuits – one of many... (NCERT Class 12 Physics Ch7 pg 1)
